sentences.text: No.
No, I didn't see her.
I never knew that my aunt is there.
She just got that few words by coincidence.
Oh, approximately 10 days.
Yeah.
Yes.
Well after arrival and selection, we went off.
We walked about 10 minutes to a-- to a center, which-- which we had to discard all our clothing in a yard.
And I remember we-- we could keep shoes and belts around.
But I forgot my belt on-- on my pants.
And they wouldn't let me go back to the heap, for the huge heap already was three, four meter high, full of pants and jackets.
And they wouldn't let me there.
So I found a pair of tefillin And I cut the leather off and use it for a belt for a few weeks, until I got a belt in Germany.
No, those prison-- they were giving us striped prison pants.
And they--
Nothing.
Which-- well, you couldn't choose.
Now after that, they were shearing all the hair off everybody.
They were prisoners, too.
Yeah.
Prisoners who are working.
That was very proximity of the crematorium.
And they-- we didn't know all these details.
But some of them were-- were joking, telling, you don't worry-- you go in through the door, you come out through the chimney.
And they were laughing.
But they knew that--
No, they knew we were sent to work.
But they were telling you.
No.
And, well, after I got my shoes and my improvised belt, and we went to have a shower.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to submerge the shoes or belts in a carbolic solution, little cement bars.
And some of the staffs, there were girls there working.
That was actually a Jewish girl, a Polish Jewish girl, who had a sort of a sponge with a solution.
And they put on everyone's head to disinfect his head, as well.
Shaven.
And then we had a shower, a hot shower with some soap in there.
And we have soap.
And after the shower, they handed also prisoners, they handed this prison clothing.
But we didn't have enough time.
So told us we were given short sleeves and-- and short pants, and we could change with another one if we were lucky.
If not, you just have to bear it.
And we were taken to the gypsy camp for the night.
The gypsies were in charge, yeah.
And those gypsies were-- were with their families.
Each family had a little room in the barrack.
Barrack accommodated about between 600 and 800 people.
They were horse barracks from the First World War, concreted.

And they even had the heating facilities for the horses, very long brick meter high in the middle, which were actually fired two hands with wood or coal, and they had two little four-inch chimneys going out from every barrack.
No.
They warmed-- in the First World War, the horses were warmed.
But--
We were not-- it wasn't cold at that time anyway.
But the gypsies came middle of the night.
He couldn't sleep.
Some people wanted to go to toilets, or-- and they only took a lot of people to the toilet, there were 20 together.
And the toilets were a separate barrack, which-- which was long, cement slabs, which had about half inch steel [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in about 10 inch deep from the cement level.
So nobody can jump and escape through.
Down was a real dam that could have been six, or eight-foot diameter with cement, where the water flew like a river permanently.
Everything washed away.
But that water could have been two foot deep as you looked in it.
But to get down there, you-- you would have had to cut heavy steel in every toilet hole.
And so when the gypsies couldn't sleep, they came out and beat some of the fellows up with long sticks.
And he was working on that brick-built stove in the middle, which was about two and a half foot from the ground high.
And people were sleeping inside each other in a row on the cement.
It was hard to get out, really, from there.
You could sit on the next person was sleeping between your knees.
No bunks there.
And these bunks, you-- you wouldn't put more than 200 people in that.
They would pack 600 to 800 in it.
Never stretch out in that place.
Some fellows went up on the crossbeam, and they tied themselves with their belts so-- so that when they fall asleep, so they won't fall down.
To the rafters, yeah.
There was feet hanging, with-- with a hand tied to it.
You would see them sleeping like that.
Food was very scarce.
They-- in the morning, they gave you-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some they called it tea, some dark, dark water.
But it was very hot, warm.
And the dishes they requisitioned from the railways which came-- people brought different sized dishes.
Two three, up to 10 liters, some of them.
And they used to fill it and give it to groups.
And the groups had to-- same for-- for dinner or lunch, whatever soup they gave.
And groups, to make it equitable, they were counting your Adam--
--apple.
How many times you swallow.
Five times each.
Had to go five times until the thing is finished if they had a two or five liter dish with food.
Nobody took extra.
Yeah.
We get zuppa.
They call it bunker soup.

That's a-- a semolina soup with bits of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vegetables-- some dry carrots or sugar beet, dry sugar-- after the sugar is extracted from the sugar beet, the left is given-- they feed animals with it.
So they put that in.
And-- and we got a slice of bread sometimes in the evening.
Just one slice, yeah.
And they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that bunker zuppa had some bits of meat.
You didn't know what meat it was, but it was very watery, the whole thing.
Yes.
They-- they-- they-- many got weak.
And I saw-- I saw people shaving with finding a spoon.
Well, nobody had a spoon, but some were brought in somehow.
And they were not used to eat.
The handle was sharpened on the cement so they could shave with it.
And the one was showing the other.
Some-- most of them wouldn't shave at all.
With me.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] every-- every morning and evening, they made appeals, the Germans.
And it took about 40, 45 minutes before they counted so many barracks.
And we were standing outside, and two Germans came in with one prisoner local, and he went with the three like a group.
And they wrote down barrack number 17, whatever, 600 people, and counted them.
No one really could escape anymore here.
There were electrified wires around.
And between the two camps were also electrified the wire.
Before the electric wire, you had--
No, no.
I mean Birkenau itself was divided where-- where these children were, and then the women's camp.
And between those camps, also electric wire were running.
You couldn't get through.
And you couldn't not only handed-- you couldn't handed a letter over.
You had to throw it with a stone attached to it, because the electrified fence had a small warning fence, which-- which was only about 4-foot high, of wire.
But you could go through that.
That was only-- the wire to carry the warning, maybe, a little plate, 6,000 volts or whatever it was.
And on the other side of the fence was the same.
So you had actually three fences.
Well, I-- I thought-- by that time,  you knew what's going on in the place.
Because we saw the crematoriums.
Nothing in Birkenau.
We were just sitting, you know, go in the yard, and twice appear.
And actually, I met a-- a cousin who was from [NON-ENGLISH], and told him about what the Polish man told us.
And we want to be in that transport if we can.
So I'm leaving the barrack, as all get up very early before the counting, and moving into the first row of the first barrack.
Because if they cut off 1,500, and that's it.
That's what happened.
And nobody knew which barrack you are, because we were all in these pajamas.
Yeah.
Nobody had names there.
No numbers, no names.
And they wanted 1,500, so they got 1,500.
And I just told my father, he saw the same, and it can't be worse than here.
You do nothing, no food.
And you see, you-- you can see the crematoriums.
And these people in charge, they already knew.
They told us what's going on.
So that known very quickly after we arrived.
No.
They-- they're telling us there's gas chambers there.
And they--
And crematorium.
They gassing the people and they burning them.
And that-- that's-- after ten days you knew every detail.
You even knew-- knew that those people who were working and distributing stuff for us, they also staff of the crematorium.
And after three months they were put in themselves.
They had a good life for three months.
They were given extra food and privileges.
But three months, they were killed.
And another staff took over, which also got killed.
Apparently the last lot might have survived part of it, when the Russians run-- run over.
In Birkenau, only beating by these gypsies.
The German guard only came in when they counted us.
But we saw people who died by committing suicide.
They went through the first little fence, and they touching the wires.
They were collected on stretchers and taken out of the camp by other prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's-- they knew when they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were cut off next morning, the 1,500, we was taken out to the further out of camp.
That means the square.
See, the camp was divided a long way, different camps, maybe five or six different camps.
We were not in the last one, but the one next.
That was the Zigeurnerlager.
On cots, yeah.
And then they put them on cots with rubber wheels pushed by prisoners, taken out from the camp.
But they died from weakness.
We were in that camp only two days in that F Lager.
And then we were given a half a loaf of bread, and a piece of margarina, and-- and a blanket.
Well in the gypsy lager, no.
How could they miss 1,500 when every day they sending transports all over the place into Germany into different Polish places?
A new arrival coming everyday.
Nobody knew.
Yeah, well the Germans took us over.
That was a transport which they send over, the 1,500.
Only this Polish man told us about it.
Well, it was unusual, because we-- we didn't even know they are gypsies for the first, because they were normal Germans to us.
They were all German gypsies.
No.
They were prisoners, but in their civilian clothes.
And these gypsies were actually gassed and killed around September 1944.
And when they took them onto tracks, that was a panic, because they knew exactly what's going on.
And they were together with their children in-- in those little rooms.
I wasn't there at time.
I heard about it.
Yes, yes.
This [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] young man-- now he is not young anymore-- called Herschel Silberstein.
He lives in [NON-ENGLISH].
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pearl Lazar.
He lives in [NON-ENGLISH].
Now Pearl Lazar had also his father with him.
He died in-- in Allach from weakness, starvation.
Yeah. he-- he was heavy smoker.
And he gave his own bread away for cigarettes.
And anyone who did that, three months, he was finished.
He didn't have a chance.
And we were given cigarettes in-- in those camps in Allach.
[NON-ENGLISH], Yugoslav cigarettes, 60 a month, two per day.
I never ever sold cigarettes to fellow prisoners.
Only to the guards, to the Germans.
Germans didn't have enough cigarettes.
They bought it.
And they actually gave you more bread than the prisoners could give you.
But the first few days, the price of bread was you had to give too-- too many cigarettes for a bread.
If you waited a week, you could hide your cigarettes, then you made a much better deal.
It was 12 cigarettes for a bread.
In a week's time you-- you get bread for eight cigarettes, because the cigarette stock went down.
No.
Well in Allach, you could hide it
Yeah, afterwards.
In your bed, in your mat, under your mattress, or whatever.
In Birkenau nobody gave you cigarettes.
No, no pockets, no.
And we were taken to the trains in-- in Birkenau from F Lager, and given that bread and a bit of food, and put into wagons, unload the wagons
Cattle cars.
But each car-- each car had two German soldiers with us-- guards--
Inside.
The German soldiers had straw.
So they had a bit soft.
And we didn't have straw.
So I actually, maybe my father was next to that guard, because they occupied the center where the doors were.
And he decided the door, they were only putting 40 people in, not 80. 20 on either side.
So even I've got a bit of food from them on the way.
I was too young, so they felt sorry for me.
And they were old Germans, not SS guard.
They were in their 50s.
We came-- we went through Katowice, Gliwice.
It's a double locomotive, very fast.
And come through Vienna.
[NON-ENGLISH] to Vienna, was, I remember Vienna got bombed in the night.
When we came there in the morning it was smoldering, and all the station were half in ruins.
You could see fire brigades and pipes everywhere, water sprinkling.
And we were only there because some tracks were still operational.
So there-- there was some holdup.
We were there about half an hour, no more.
Then, off we went.
Oh that was probably-- couldn't give an exact date.
But it was on the-- must have been beginning of June
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '44 June.
And we went-- we came to Dachau, not to the concentration camp, to the railway station, which is a small German station with three, four platforms.
And 500 of us were cut off, disembark from there.
And 1,000 off to concentration camp in New Dachau.
And we were wait-- a German, there was an SS officer, a lieutenant, in whole uniform, leather jacket, leather long coat, and motorbike.
I remember a 250cc BMW in German military colors.
No.
And-- and he had been a few guards there, and he had a short speech that I understand German a bit from what I learned in school.
And he said, you're going to work in this place, and you'll be well fed.
We already-- we thought we would be fed before killed, like--
--Like the fellows in the jungle, then eat us!
Feed you before killing you.
Then we went off.
From there, the guards, 500, to-- we walked through nicely wheat fields.
The wheat was still on the field, getting yellow slightly.
And all this good smell of-- of wild flowers in the wheat field.
We came to a camp about an hour's walk.
There's no houses in the way.
Nothing.
And that camp, as we approached, we saw that it got no electric fences.
Just ordinary wire fence, which-- what they use?
How you call that wire?
Barbed.
Barbed wire.
Thanks.
And four towers, it was a small camp with four guard towers on the four-- thing.
And on a gate also from barbed wired wooden gate.
We came in the camp, and we had to discard the clothing.
You were given fresh ones.
Striped clothing.
After having a shower and get clothing, we get eight in a room.
It was smaller rooms.
And now-- we're now three-- three people on the one above you.
So it was eight bunks in the room.
That's 16 people.
And after that--
Yeah, with me.
And after that, they told us we could come.
We had a-- that was a square sort of a camp-- no barracks.
Only the barracks were interconnecting with each other, and they had a kitchen, and a sort of a dining room.
And we went-- they said to come to a dining room.
We never had dining room.
And they had benches there with long tables, and with sliced army bread in baskets.
And there were-- we-- we could eat as much as we wanted.
And they had this bunker zuppa very thick with meat.
I was-- I had hungry for a few weeks.
And that had a speech, of course, when we take as another three, four days before we were-- we were go to proper work.
But I was-- in appeal, I was taken by this officer.
He asked--
On the bike.
He-- he made the appeal.
On the parade.
And he just pulled me over, and told me to clean up his little office.
His office was inside the camp.
And-- and he had a shortwave radio, medium-wave and shortwave.
I wouldn't dare listening to it.
And the whole room was only about 2 by 3 meter, with an-- with an office desk, a telephone, and reading lamp.
And he had the the Nazi party's paper, with the red print on top.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] was the Munich German newspaper.
And they already had the-- the invasion of France was 6th of June.
Could have been a few days-- one or two days after that.
So after I cleaned up, he told me where was the water, and the mop.
Mopped up, emptied his paper bin.
He gave me half a [NON-ENGLISH]-- that's a German butter-- and a piece of white bread.
And off I went.
Did you read the newspaper first?
Just the headlines.
I couldn't make it out.
Well, it-- it was complete change for the last three weeks, and the ghetto.
So it's not that-- not our hope, we didn't go up.
It's just we became a bit careless, like worryless.
Forget worrying.
Whatever.
We won't survive.
Whatever we can have now is good.
You make the best of it.
And next morning we went on appeal.
And there, this officer ask, he needs ten young fellows who have their fathers here.
There were more than 10.
So I didn't know to go or not.
So my father was next to me on appeal, and he said, go.
We didn't know, what can they do?
They make sausages out of us or what?
So we went out, and the rest was discharged.
And they told us, you-- tomorrow morning, 7 o'clock you going to work in Dachau in the-- in the city, in the township, and no guards with you.
If you run away, your father gets shot.
So that was it.

We-- we had a-- we had a very good working place on a food store of the Army in the third organization, which was housed in-- in the roofs of requisitioned roofs of a number of buildings, which were all about three floor high, and slightly sloping, so they had a-- a little two-foot high brick wall between one building ends and the next one.
But the roof was one over the whole lot.
And the goods arriving there were very bulky stuff-- sugar, and rice, and flour, and some goodies, too.
But these were mainly in 50 kilo or-- or larger bags.
And to take them up, we had a crane with a steel rope, and then a little electric hoist operated.
From the truck, truck moved in--
And we had to move it up through a shaft into the roof section.
And inside the roof, we had trolleys, because different material were in different places.
And you couldn't move the trolley from one house to the next because of these two-foot brick walls.
So you had to take another trolley and repack them maybe two or three times.
Some goods were on the same place, which we were lucky.
Of course the-- the manager told us you can eat cheese, sardines, anything open.
But don't take anything into the camp.
So we-- we had even wafers there.
Military stuff.
Lunch-- lunch hour, we-- we got-- we got 16 liters sent out from the camp, hot food.
But we didn't even touch it while we here.
We-- we didn't want to take it back to the camp, so they wouldn't find out that we eat all the goodies.
We throw them in the rubbish, on the gutter down, the 16 liter.
We took back the empty, that was insulated like a thermos, for 20 liter ones.
And the work was real heavy.
We-- we could-- we were there about 10 days, two weeks.
And then they canceled us.
We were weak-- too weak.
They wanted stronger men.
And they took stronger ones with guards.
So we ran back to other groups.
Back to the camp.
Rotschweig.
R-O-T-H-S-- no, sorry, R-O-T-S-C-H-W-E-I-G.
Rotschweig.
That is called where the camp was.
But we didn't see any locality or village under this name.
